1:
"Now these are the names of the children of Israel, which came into Egypt; every man and his household came with Jacob." The witnesses note the names of Israel's house that came into Egypt. On "these are the names of the children of Israel, which came into Egypt," the grammatical voices note the link. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "these are the names of the children of Israel, which came into Egypt; every man and his household came with Jacob" — the opening of Exodus repeats the list of those who went down to Egypt (Genesis 46:8-27), binding Exodus to Genesis; the names of the tribal heads; the covenant family entering Egypt (the seventy souls, v.5); the foundation of the nation about to be born.

On "every man and his household came with Jacob," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the household. Calvin and Henry note that each came "with his household" (with Jacob) — the whole covenant family removing to Egypt (Genesis 46:6-7); the household of faith; the seed from which the great nation would grow.

On the link, the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the continuity: the repeating of the names (Genesis 46) — Exodus continuing the story of Genesis (the providence over the covenant family); the seventy who went down now to multiply into a nation (v.7); the bridge from the patriarchs to the people. The verse displays the names of Israel's house that came into Egypt.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the link — "these are the names of the children of Israel, which came into Egypt," the list (Genesis 46:8, Keil, Gill); "every man and his household came with Jacob," the household (Genesis 46:6, Calvin, Henry); the continuity (v.7, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the entering house. The verse marks the names: of Israel. The verse displays the households: with Jacob. The verse displays the link: to Genesis. The verse displays the nation: to come. The names display the covenant family entering Egypt. The verse displays the Israel: entering. The verse displays the Jacob: with his house. The verse displays the seed: to multiply. The names of Israel's house that came into Egypt display the link from Genesis: the children of Israel came into Egypt, every man with his household (Genesis 46:8, 6-7) — the covenant family, the seed of the nation about to be born. The verse displays the enumerated sons (vv.2-5).
2:
"Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah," The very brief verse notes the first four sons of Jacob by Leah. The grammatical voices note the names. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah" — the first four sons of Jacob (by Leah) are named: Reuben (the firstborn), Simeon, Levi, and Judah; the heads of four of the tribes; the genealogy (Genesis 46:8-15); the line of Leah's first sons (Levi the priestly tribe, Judah the royal and Messianic, Genesis 49:10).

On "Reuben, Simeon, Levi," the witnesses note the sons. Gill notes the first three of Leah (Reuben, Simeon, Levi) — the elder sons; the tribes of Israel; the genealogy.

On "and Judah," the grammatical-historical note the royal tribe: "and Judah" — the fourth son (Leah's), the royal and Messianic tribe (Genesis 49:10; from which Christ would come, Matthew 1:2-3; Hebrews 7:14); the line of promise; the chief of the tribes. The verse displays the first four sons of Jacob.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the names — "Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah," the four sons (Genesis 46:8, Keil, Gill); "Reuben, Simeon, Levi," the elder sons (Gill); "and Judah," the royal tribe (Genesis 49:10, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named sons. The verse marks the Reuben: firstborn. The verse displays the Levi: priestly. The verse displays the Judah: royal. The verse displays the tribes: enumerated. The names display the first sons of Leah. The verse displays the sons: four. The verse displays the Judah: the Messianic line. The verse displays the genealogy: continuing. The first four sons of Jacob by Leah display the tribal heads: Reuben, Simeon, Levi, and Judah (Genesis 46:8; 49:10) — the elder sons, Levi the priestly and Judah the royal Messianic tribe. The verse displays the remaining sons (vv.3-4). That the inspired writer opens the book of the redemption with this roll of the tribal fathers binds the deliverance of the nation to the covenant made with the patriarchs (Genesis 35:23-26), so that the Exodus is read from the first as the keeping of God's oath to Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob (Genesis 15:13-16).
3:
"Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin," The very brief verse notes three more sons of Jacob. The grammatical voices note the names. JFB and Gill note that "Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin" — three more sons of Jacob: Issachar and Zebulun (Leah's last two sons) and Benjamin (Rachel's younger son); the heads of three tribes; the genealogy (Genesis 46:13-14, 21); the continuing list.

On "Issachar, Zebulun," the witnesses note Leah's sons. Gill notes Issachar and Zebulun (the last of Leah's six sons) — the tribes; the genealogy.

On "and Benjamin," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note Rachel's son: "and Benjamin" — Rachel's younger son (Joseph being already in Egypt, v.5); the tribe of Benjamin (Genesis 46:21); the line of Rachel; the genealogy. The verse displays three more sons of Jacob.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the names — "Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin," the three sons (Genesis 46:13, JFB, Gill); "Issachar, Zebulun," Leah's sons (Gill); "and Benjamin," Rachel's son (Genesis 46:21, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named sons. The verse marks the Issachar: and Zebulun. The verse displays the Benjamin: Rachel's. The verse displays the tribes: enumerated. The verse displays the genealogy: continuing. The names display more sons of Jacob. The verse displays the sons: three. The verse displays the Benjamin: the youngest. The verse displays the genealogy: continuing. Three more sons of Jacob display the tribal heads: Issachar, Zebulun, and Benjamin (Genesis 46:13-14, 21) — Leah's last sons and Rachel's younger son. The verse displays the handmaids' sons (v.4). These tribal heads, with their brethren, are the seed in whom the promise of a great nation (Genesis 46:3) and of a numberless multitude (Genesis 15:5) would be fulfilled in the land of Egypt, the small beginning of the people God would redeem (Exodus 1:7). The order of the names, by the mothers and the birth, is the same as in the closing genealogy of Genesis (Genesis 46:8-25), so that the two books are knit together as one continuous history of the covenant family from the going-down into Egypt to the coming-up out of it.
4:
"Dan, and Naphtali, Gad, and Asher." The very brief verse notes the sons of the handmaids. The grammatical voices note the names. Gill notes that "Dan, and Naphtali, Gad, and Asher" — the four sons of the handmaids: Dan and Naphtali (Bilhah's sons) and Gad and Asher (Zilpah's sons); the heads of four tribes; the genealogy (Genesis 46:16-25); the completing of the eleven sons named (Joseph already in Egypt, v.5).

On "Dan, and Naphtali," the witnesses note Bilhah's sons. Gill notes Dan and Naphtali (Bilhah's, Rachel's handmaid) — the tribes; the genealogy.

On "Gad, and Asher," the grammatical-historical note Zilpah's sons: "Gad, and Asher" — Zilpah's (Leah's handmaid) sons; the tribes (Genesis 46:16); the handmaids' seed reckoned among the tribes; the genealogy complete (eleven named, Joseph in Egypt). The verse displays the sons of the handmaids.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the names — "Dan, and Naphtali, Gad, and Asher," the handmaids' sons (Genesis 46:16, Gill); "Dan, and Naphtali," Bilhah's (Gill); "Gad, and Asher," Zilpah's (Genesis 46:16, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the named sons. The verse marks the Dan: and Naphtali. The verse displays the Gad: and Asher. The verse displays the tribes: enumerated. The verse displays the genealogy: complete. The names display the handmaids' sons. The verse displays the sons: four. The verse displays the handmaids: their seed. The verse displays the genealogy: complete. The sons of the handmaids display the tribal heads: Dan, Naphtali, Gad, and Asher (Genesis 46:16-25) — Bilhah's and Zilpah's sons, the handmaids' seed among the tribes. The verse displays the sum of the seventy (v.5). That the sons of the handmaids are set down with the sons of the wives shows that the whole house of Israel, without distinction, was reckoned among the covenant people whom God would bring up out of Egypt (Genesis 46:25-27), every tribe alike the object of the promise (Genesis 28:14). Thus the twelve sons of Jacob, by Leah and Rachel and their handmaids, stand at the head of the book as the fathers of the tribes that would be marshalled out of Egypt as the armies of the LORD (Exodus 12:41), the whole nation present in seed in these few names.
5:
"And all the souls that came out of the loins of Jacob were seventy souls: for Joseph was in Egypt already." The witnesses note the seventy souls of Jacob's house. On "all the souls that came out of the loins of Jacob were seventy souls," the grammatical voices note the number. Ellicott and Gill note that "all the souls that came out of the loins of Jacob were seventy souls" — the house of Jacob that went down to Egypt numbered seventy (Genesis 46:27; Deuteronomy 10:22); the small seed from which the great nation would grow (v.7); the covenant family; the number of completeness.

On "seventy souls," the reformed and witnesses note the small beginning. Gill notes the seventy (the small number, Genesis 46:27) — the seed from which God would make a great nation (Genesis 46:3); the small beginning of the multitude (the stars of heaven, Deuteronomy 10:22); the providence multiplying the seed.

On "for Joseph was in Egypt already," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note Joseph: "for Joseph was in Egypt already" — Joseph (and his two sons) already in Egypt (not among those who "came"); the providence (Joseph sent before, Genesis 45:5-8); the count completing the seventy; the saviour already there. The verse displays the seventy souls of Jacob's house.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the number — "all the souls that came out of the loins of Jacob were seventy souls," the seventy (Genesis 46:27, Ellicott, Gill); "seventy souls," the small seed (Deuteronomy 10:22, Gill); "for Joseph was in Egypt already," Joseph (Genesis 45:5, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the seventy. The verse marks the souls: seventy. The verse displays the seed: small. The verse displays the Joseph: already there. The verse displays the nation: to grow. The number displays the small seed of the great nation. The verse displays the souls: seventy. The verse displays the providence: at work. The verse displays the multitude: to come. The seventy souls of Jacob's house display the small seed: seventy souls, Joseph already in Egypt (Genesis 46:27; Deuteronomy 10:22; 45:5) — the small beginning from which God would make a great nation. The verse displays the dying generation (v.6).
6:
"And Joseph died, and all his brethren, and all that generation." The very brief verse notes the death of Joseph's generation. The grammatical voices note the passing. Calvin and Gill note that "Joseph died, and all his brethren, and all that generation" — Joseph (Genesis 50:26), his brethren, and the whole generation that went down to Egypt died; the passing of the patriarchal generation; the setting for the change of fortune (the new king, v.8); the close of one age and the dawn of the bondage.

On "Joseph died, and all his brethren," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the death. Wesley and Clarke note the death of Joseph and his brethren (Genesis 50:26) — the generation gone; the protectors of Israel passed; the way opened for the oppression (the new king knowing not Joseph, v.8).

On "all that generation," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the transition: "all that generation" — the whole generation dead; the transition from favor to bondage; the providence (the death of Joseph removing the shield, the affliction beginning, that the deliverance might come, the covenant kept); the dawn of the long bondage (foretold, Genesis 15:13). The verse displays the death of Joseph's generation.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the passing — "Joseph died, and all his brethren, and all that generation," the death (Genesis 50:26, Calvin, Gill); "Joseph died, and all his brethren," the generation (Wesley, Clarke); "all that generation," the transition (Genesis 15:13, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dying generation. The verse marks the Joseph: died. The verse displays the brethren: dead. The verse displays the generation: passed. The verse displays the bondage: to come. The death displays the passing of the patriarchal age. The verse displays the Joseph: gone. The verse displays the generation: ended. The verse displays the transition: to bondage. The death of Joseph's generation displays the transition to bondage: Joseph and all that generation died (Genesis 50:26; 15:13) — the protectors gone, the way opened for the oppression. The verse displays the multiplying Israel (v.7).
7:
"And the children of Israel were fruitful, and increased abundantly, and multiplied, and waxed exceeding mighty; and the land was filled with them." The witnesses note Israel's great increase fulfilling the promise. On "the children of Israel were fruitful, and increased abundantly, and multiplied," the grammatical voices note the increase. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the children of Israel were fruitful, and increased abundantly, and multiplied, and waxed exceeding mighty" — Israel grew exceedingly in Egypt; the heaping of words (fruitful, increased, multiplied, mighty) marking the extraordinary, God-given increase; the fulfilling of the promise (Genesis 46:3, "I will make of thee a great nation"; the covenant of multiplication, Genesis 12:2; 15:5; 17:6).

On "increased abundantly," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the providence. Calvin and Ellicott note the abundant increase (the swarming, teeming multiplication, like the increase of living creatures, Genesis 1:20-22) — the providence multiplying the seed; the covenant promise fulfilled; the small seed (seventy, v.5) becoming a multitude.

On "the land was filled with them," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the multitude: "the land was filled with them" — Goshen (and beyond) filled with Israelites; the great nation born in Egypt (the providence, Genesis 15:13-14); the increase that provoked the new king's fear (v.9); the covenant promise visibly fulfilled. The verse displays Israel's great increase fulfilling the promise.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the increase — "the children of Israel were fruitful, and increased abundantly, and multiplied, and waxed exceeding mighty," the great increase (Genesis 46:3, Keil, Gill); "increased abundantly," the providence (Genesis 1:20, Calvin, Ellicott); "the land was filled with them," the multitude (Genesis 15:13, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the multiplying Israel. The verse marks the increase: abundant. The verse displays the multitude: mighty. The verse displays the land: filled. The verse displays the promise: fulfilled. The increase displays the covenant promise fulfilled. The verse displays the Israel: multiplying. The verse displays the seed: a multitude. The verse displays the providence: at work. Israel's great increase fulfilling the promise displays the covenant: Israel was fruitful, multiplied, and filled the land (Genesis 46:3; 12:2; 15:5) — the small seed become a mighty nation, the promise visibly fulfilled. The verse displays the multiplied Israel, feared by the new king (vv.8-9).
8:
"Now there arose up a new king over Egypt, which knew not Joseph." The witnesses note the new king who knew not Joseph — the crux of the turning tide. On "there arose up a new king over Egypt," the grammatical voices note the change. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "there arose up a new king over Egypt, which knew not Joseph" — a new king (the Hebrew may mean a king of a new line or dynasty, "arose," rose up, perhaps a usurper or a new royal house) came to power in Egypt; the change of rulers; the turning of Israel's fortunes (from favor under Joseph's Pharaoh to bondage under the new king); the setting of the oppression.

On "a new king," the reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the dynasty. Calvin and Ellicott note that the "new king" was likely of a new dynasty (the majority view; perhaps the change after the Hyksos, or a native dynasty hostile to the foreign-favoring past) — the new royal house; the change of policy toward Israel; the king who owed nothing to Joseph.

On "which knew not Joseph," interpreters expound the ingratitude. The puritan Henry and the dominant reading note "which knew not Joseph" — the new king "knew not" Joseph: either he was truly ignorant of Joseph's history (the deliverer of Egypt, Genesis 41), the long years and the new dynasty having effaced the memory; or, more pointedly, he "knew not" in the sense of regarding not, acknowledging not (ignoring or refusing to honor) Joseph's services and the debt Egypt owed him; the base ingratitude of the world (forgetting its benefactors, Ecclesiastes 9:15); the providence turning the tide (the favor forgotten, the bondage begun, that God might in time deliver, fulfilling Genesis 15:13-14); the warning of how soon the world forgets.

On the new king, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the providence: the new king's rise (and ignorance of Joseph) — the providence beginning the bondage foretold (Genesis 15:13); the affliction that would make Egypt hateful and Canaan dear; the turning of the tide that set the stage for the Exodus. The verse displays the new king who knew not Joseph.

The witnesses agree the substance: a new king (likely of a new dynasty) arose who knew not Joseph (Keil, Calvin, Henry, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of the turning tide: "a new king... which knew not Joseph" — the change of rulers turning Israel's fortunes from favor to bondage; "knew not Joseph" either truly ignorant, or (more pointedly) ignoring and refusing to honor Joseph's services — the base ingratitude of the world that forgets its benefactors (Ecclesiastes 9:15); the providence beginning the foretold bondage (Genesis 15:13-14), the stage set for the Exodus. The verse displays the new-king crux. The verse marks the king: new. The verse displays the ignorance: knew not Joseph. The verse displays the ingratitude: of the world. The verse displays the providence: the tide turning. The new king displays the turning of Israel's fortunes. The verse displays the king: arisen. The verse displays the Joseph: forgotten. The verse displays the bondage: beginning. The new king who knew not Joseph displays the crux: a new king arose which knew not Joseph (Ecclesiastes 9:15; Genesis 15:13-14) — the change of rulers turning favor to bondage, the world's ingratitude forgetting its benefactor, the providence beginning the foretold affliction. The verse displays the scheming king (vv.9-10).
9:
"And he said unto his people, Behold, the people of the children of Israel are more and mightier than we:" The witnesses note Pharaoh's alarm at Israel's growth. On "he said unto his people, Behold, the people of the children of Israel are more and mightier than we," the grammatical voices note the alarm. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "he said unto his people, Behold, the people of the children of Israel are more and mightier than we" — the new king, alarmed at Israel's great increase (v.7), warned his people that the Israelites had grown more numerous and mighty than the Egyptians (an exaggeration, or a fear of their strength in Goshen); the jealous alarm; the pretext for the oppression (vv.10-11).

On "more and mightier than we," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the fear. Calvin and Ellicott note the king's fear (the Israelites grown so numerous and strong as to seem a threat) — the jealousy of the multiplying people; the alarm (real or pretended) that bred the policy of oppression.

On the alarm, the grammatical-historical note the pretext: Pharaoh's words (the alarm at Israel's strength) — the pretext for the bondage (the fear of a fifth column, v.10); the providence (the very increase that fulfilled the promise, v.7, now made the occasion of affliction, that God might deliver); the scheming against the covenant people. The verse displays Pharaoh's alarm at Israel's growth.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the alarm — "Behold, the people of the children of Israel are more and mightier than we," the fear (v.7, Keil, Gill); "more and mightier than we," the jealousy (Calvin, Ellicott); the pretext (v.10, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the alarmed king. The verse marks the alarm: Israel mighty. The verse displays the fear: of their strength. The verse displays the pretext: forming. The verse displays the oppression: to come. The alarm displays Pharaoh's jealous fear of Israel. The verse displays the king: alarmed. The verse displays the Israel: feared. The verse displays the scheme: forming. Pharaoh's alarm at Israel's growth displays the pretext for oppression: the people of Israel are more and mightier than we (v.7) — the jealous fear of the multiplying people, the pretext for the bondage. The verse displays the scheming king (v.10).
10:
"Come on, let us deal wisely with them; lest they multiply, and it come to pass, that, when there falleth out any war, they join also unto our enemies, and fight against us, and so get them up out of the land." The witnesses note Pharaoh's crafty policy against Israel. On "Come on, let us deal wisely with them," the grammatical voices note the craft. Ellicott and Gill note that "Come on, let us deal wisely with them; lest they multiply" — Pharaoh proposed to "deal wisely" (craftily, subtly) with Israel, to check their increase; the politic cunning (the worldly "wisdom" against God's people); the scheme to oppress them (vv.11-14); the craft that God would turn to folly (1 Corinthians 3:19, "the wisdom of this world is foolishness with God").

On "lest they multiply... they join also unto our enemies," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the fear. Wesley and Clarke note Pharaoh's pretext — the fear that Israel, multiplying, might in war join Egypt's enemies and rise against them, or leave the land; the political fear (the multiplying foreigners a supposed danger); the pretext for the oppression.

On "and so get them up out of the land," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the irony: "and so get them up out of the land" — Pharaoh feared Israel would leave Egypt (depriving him of their labor); the irony (the very thing he feared, their going up, God would bring to pass in the Exodus, but by God's hand, not their flight); the providence (man's craft against God's purpose turned to folly, the oppression hastening the deliverance). The verse displays Pharaoh's crafty policy against Israel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the craft — "let us deal wisely with them; lest they multiply," the cunning (1 Corinthians 3:19, Ellicott, Gill); "they join also unto our enemies," the fear (Wesley, Clarke); "and so get them up out of the land," the irony (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the scheming king. The verse marks the craft: deal wisely. The verse displays the fear: lest they multiply. The verse displays the pretext: war. The verse displays the irony: get up out of the land. The policy displays Pharaoh's craft against God's people. The verse displays the king: scheming. The verse displays the Israel: targeted. The verse displays the providence: to overrule. Pharaoh's crafty policy against Israel displays the worldly wisdom turned to folly: let us deal wisely, lest they multiply and get up out of the land (1 Corinthians 3:19) — the craft against God's people, the very thing he feared brought to pass by God in the Exodus. The verse displays the imposed taskmasters (v.11).
11:
"Therefore they did set over them taskmasters to afflict them with their burdens. And they built for Pharaoh treasure cities, Pithom and Raamses." The witnesses note Israel set to forced labor under taskmasters. On "they did set over them taskmasters to afflict them with their burdens," the grammatical voices note the bondage. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "they did set over them taskmasters to afflict them with their burdens" — the Egyptians set taskmasters (slave-drivers, overseers of forced labor) over Israel, to afflict them with hard burdens; the beginning of the bondage; the forced labor (the oppression foretold, Genesis 15:13, "they shall afflict them four hundred years"); the cruel servitude.

On "to afflict them with their burdens," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the affliction. Calvin and Ellicott note the affliction (the hard burdens, the forced labor designed to break their spirit and check their increase) — the cruelty of the bondage; the policy of oppression; the affliction of the covenant people.

On "they built for Pharaoh treasure cities, Pithom and Raamses," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the labor: "they built for Pharaoh treasure cities, Pithom and Raamses" — Israel forced to build store-cities (Pithom and Raamses, in the eastern Delta, for Pharaoh's provisions or arms); the hard labor in brick and building (Exodus 5:7-19); the bondage in Egypt (the iron furnace, Deuteronomy 4:20); the affliction that God would see and deliver from (Exodus 3:7). The verse displays Israel set to forced labor under taskmasters.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the bondage — "they did set over them taskmasters to afflict them with their burdens," the forced labor (Genesis 15:13, Keil, Gill); "to afflict them with their burdens," the affliction (Calvin, Ellicott); "they built for Pharaoh treasure cities, Pithom and Raamses," the labor (Deuteronomy 4:20, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the afflicted Israel. The verse marks the taskmasters: set over them. The verse displays the burdens: hard. The verse displays the cities: Pithom and Raamses. The verse displays the bondage: begun. The bondage displays Israel afflicted with forced labor. The verse displays the Israel: enslaved. The verse displays the taskmasters: cruel. The verse displays the affliction: foretold. Israel set to forced labor under taskmasters displays the bondage: taskmasters to afflict them, building Pithom and Raamses (Genesis 15:13; Deuteronomy 4:20) — the hard servitude foretold, the iron furnace from which God would deliver. The verse displays the more they were afflicted (v.12).
12:
"But the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied and grew. And they were grieved because of the children of Israel." The witnesses note Israel multiplying the more they were afflicted — the providence overruling. On "the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied and grew," the grammatical voices note the increase. Ellicott and Gill note that "the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied and grew" — the affliction, designed to check Israel's increase (v.10), had the contrary effect: the more they were oppressed, the more they multiplied and grew; the providence overruling the persecution (God blessing his people the more under affliction); the frustrating of Pharaoh's policy.

On the increase under affliction, the reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the providence. Calvin and Wesley note the divine overruling — God's blessing on Israel made the affliction serve the very end it was meant to prevent (the increase); the providence that turns persecution to the growth of the church (the blood of the martyrs the seed of the church, the church often growing most under the cross, as the bush burned but was not consumed, Exodus 3:2); the lesson — man cannot frustrate God's purpose; the more the world afflicts God's people, the more (by God's blessing) they grow.

On "they were grieved because of the children of Israel," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the vexation: "they were grieved (or, were in dread, loathed) because of the children of Israel" — the Egyptians vexed, alarmed, and filled with loathing and dread at Israel's continued increase (their policy failing); the frustration of the oppressor; the providence (the increase that fed Pharaoh's fear and hardened his cruelty, vv.13-22, yet served God's purpose). The verse displays Israel multiplying the more they were afflicted.

The witnesses agree the substance: the more Israel was afflicted, the more they multiplied, to the Egyptians' vexation (Ellicott, Calvin, Wesley, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the providence overruling: "the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied" — God overruling the persecution to serve the very end it meant to prevent, the church growing most under the cross (the bush burning unconsumed, Exodus 3:2); the lesson that man cannot frustrate God's purpose, the world's affliction (by God's blessing) increasing his people. The verse displays the providence-overruling theme. The verse marks the affliction: increased. The verse displays the increase: the more. The verse displays the providence: overruling. The verse displays the Egyptians: grieved. The increase displays God's blessing under persecution. The verse displays the Israel: growing. The verse displays the affliction: frustrated. The verse displays the providence: at work. Israel multiplying the more they were afflicted displays the providence overruling persecution: the more they afflicted them, the more they multiplied (Exodus 3:2) — God blessing his people the more under the cross, man unable to frustrate his purpose. The verse displays the harder bondage (vv.13-14).
13:
"And the Egyptians made the children of Israel to serve with rigour:" The very brief verse notes the rigorous bondage of Israel. The grammatical voices note the rigour. Ellicott and Gill note that "the Egyptians made the children of Israel to serve with rigour" — the Egyptians, frustrated by Israel's increase (v.12), made them serve with rigour (harshness, cruelty, severity); the harder bondage; the increased oppression (the affliction deepening); the cruel servitude.

On "to serve with rigour," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the cruelty. Calvin and Clarke note the "rigour" (the harsh, cruel, breaking servitude) — the increased severity (the oppressors hardening as their policy failed); the cruelty of the bondage; the affliction (the iron furnace, Deuteronomy 4:20).

On the rigour, the grammatical-historical note the affliction: the rigorous service — the breaking labor (designed to crush Israel); the cruelty that cried to heaven (Exodus 3:7-9); the providence (the affliction that ripened the deliverance, the groaning that God would hear, Exodus 2:23-25). The verse displays the rigorous bondage of Israel.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the rigour — "the Egyptians made the children of Israel to serve with rigour," the harsh bondage (v.12, Ellicott, Gill); "to serve with rigour," the cruelty (Deuteronomy 4:20, Calvin, Clarke); the affliction (Exodus 3:7, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rigorous bondage. The verse marks the service: with rigour. The verse displays the cruelty: harsh. The verse displays the oppression: deepening. The verse displays the affliction: crushing. The rigour displays the cruel servitude of Israel. The verse displays the Egyptians: oppressing. The verse displays the Israel: crushed. The verse displays the deliverance: ripening. The rigorous bondage of Israel displays the deepening affliction: the Egyptians made them serve with rigour (Deuteronomy 4:20; Exodus 3:7) — the harsh, breaking servitude, the cruelty that cried to heaven. The verse displays the bitter lives (v.14). That the bondage grew harder as the people grew more numerous shows how the failing of Pharaoh's policy (Exodus 1:12) drove the oppressors to ever greater cruelty, the affliction deepening toward the very extremity in which God would hear the cry of his people and come down to deliver them (Exodus 3:7-8).
14:
"And they made their lives bitter with hard bondage, in morter, and in brick, and in all manner of service in the field: all their service, wherein they made them serve, was with rigour." The witnesses note Israel's lives made bitter with hard bondage. On "they made their lives bitter with hard bondage," the grammatical voices note the bitterness. Ellicott and Gill note that "they made their lives bitter with hard bondage, in morter, and in brick" — the Egyptians made Israel's lives bitter with hard labor: in mortar and brick (the building of the cities, v.11; the brick-making, Exodus 5:7-19) and all field-service; the bitter bondage; the crushing toil (the bitterness, remembered in the bitter herbs of the Passover, Exodus 12:8).

On "in morter, and in brick, and in all manner of service in the field," the reformed and witnesses note the labor. Gill notes the hard labor (mortar, brick, field-work) — the varied, crushing servitude; the bondage in all things; the iron furnace (Deuteronomy 4:20).

On "all their service... was with rigour," the grammatical-historical note the rigour: "all their service, wherein they made them serve, was with rigour" — the whole servitude marked by rigour (harshness, cruelty); the bitter bondage (the affliction that God would see, Exodus 3:7); the providence (the bitterness that made the deliverance sweet, and Egypt hateful); the groaning that rose to God (Exodus 2:23-25). The verse displays Israel's lives made bitter with hard bondage.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the bitterness — "they made their lives bitter with hard bondage, in morter, and in brick," the bitter labor (Exodus 12:8, Ellicott, Gill); "in morter, and in brick, and in all manner of service in the field," the labor (Deuteronomy 4:20, Gill); "all their service... was with rigour," the rigour (Exodus 3:7, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bitter bondage. The verse marks the lives: bitter. The verse displays the labor: mortar and brick. The verse displays the service: in the field. The verse displays the rigour: throughout. The bitterness displays the crushing bondage of Israel. The verse displays the Israel: in bitterness. The verse displays the bondage: hard. The verse displays the deliverance: to come. Israel's lives made bitter with hard bondage display the bitter servitude: their lives made bitter in mortar and brick, all with rigour (Exodus 12:8; 3:7) — the crushing toil, the bitterness that rose as a groaning to God. The verse displays the midwives charged (vv.15-16).
15:
"And the king of Egypt spake to the Hebrew midwives, of which the name of the one was Shiphrah, and the name of the other Puah:" The witnesses note Pharaoh charging the Hebrew midwives. On "the king of Egypt spake to the Hebrew midwives," the grammatical voices note the charge. Henry and Gill note that "the king of Egypt spake to the Hebrew midwives, of which the name of the one was Shiphrah, and the name of the other Puah" — Pharaoh, the labor-policy failing (v.12), turned to a more murderous plan: he charged the Hebrew midwives (Shiphrah and Puah, perhaps the chief of the midwives, representing many) to kill the male children at birth (v.16); the new, secret cruelty; the attempt at genocide.

On "the Hebrew midwives," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the women. Calvin and Ellicott note the midwives (Hebrew women, or Egyptian midwives to the Hebrews; the names preserved, Shiphrah and Puah, honored for their fear of God, v.17) — the women charged with the cruel task; the chief midwives (over many); the instruments Pharaoh sought to use.

On the charge, the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the cruelty: Pharaoh's charge to the midwives (the secret murder of the male infants) — the escalation of the cruelty (from hard labor to infanticide); the providence (the plot that God would frustrate through the midwives' fear, vv.17-21); the rage of the dragon against the seed (a foreshadowing of Herod, Matthew 2:16, and the dragon, Revelation 12:4). The verse displays Pharaoh charging the Hebrew midwives.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the charge — "the king of Egypt spake to the Hebrew midwives... Shiphrah... Puah," the cruel plan (v.16, Henry, Gill); "the Hebrew midwives," the women (v.17, Calvin, Ellicott); the cruelty (Matthew 2:16, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the charging king. The verse marks the midwives: Shiphrah and Puah. The verse displays the charge: the secret murder. The verse displays the cruelty: escalating. The verse displays the providence: to frustrate. The charge displays Pharaoh's murderous plot. The verse displays the king: charging. The verse displays the midwives: named. The verse displays the plot: forming. Pharaoh charging the Hebrew midwives displays the escalating cruelty: the king spake to Shiphrah and Puah (v.16; Matthew 2:16) — the murderous plot against the male children, the rage against the seed. The verse displays the cruel charge (v.16).
16:
"And he said, When ye do the office of a midwife to the Hebrew women, and see them upon the stools; if it be a son, then ye shall kill him: but if it be a daughter, then she shall live." The witnesses note Pharaoh's order to kill the Hebrew male infants. On "When ye do the office of a midwife to the Hebrew women... if it be a son, then ye shall kill him," the grammatical voices note the order. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when ye do the office of a midwife to the Hebrew women... if it be a son, then ye shall kill him: but if it be a daughter, then she shall live" — Pharaoh ordered the midwives to kill the male Hebrew infants at birth (secretly, at the stools, making it seem a stillbirth), but to let the females live; the murderous, cunning policy (the males killed to destroy the nation's strength and future, the females spared, perhaps for slaves or wives to Egyptians); the genocide of the sons.

On "see them upon the stools," the witnesses note the means. Gill notes the "stools" (the birth-stool, or the two stones; the secret means of killing at the moment of birth) — the cunning of the murder (to seem natural); the cruel device.

On "if it be a son... but if it be a daughter, then she shall live," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the design: "if it be a son, then ye shall kill him" — the killing of the males (the destruction of the nation's strength); the sparing of the females; the cunning genocide; the providence (the plot God would frustrate, vv.17-21, and overrule for the deliverer's birth, ch. 2); the rage against the seed (foreshadowing Herod, Matthew 2:16). The verse displays Pharaoh's order to kill the Hebrew male infants.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the order — "if it be a son, then ye shall kill him: but if it be a daughter, then she shall live," the genocide (Keil, Gill); "see them upon the stools," the means (Gill); the design (Matthew 2:16, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the murderous order. The verse marks the sons: to be killed. The verse displays the daughters: spared. The verse displays the stools: the means. The verse displays the genocide: cunning. The order displays Pharaoh's plot against the male children. The verse displays the king: ordering. The verse displays the sons: targeted. The verse displays the providence: to frustrate. Pharaoh's order to kill the Hebrew male infants displays the cunning genocide: kill the sons, spare the daughters (Matthew 2:16) — the destruction of the nation's strength, the plot God would frustrate. The verse displays the God-fearing midwives (v.17).
17:
"But the midwives feared God, and did not as the king of Egypt commanded them, but saved the men children alive." The witnesses note the midwives fearing God rather than the king — the crux of holy disobedience. On "the midwives feared God," interpreters expound the fear. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note "the midwives feared God" — the midwives, fearing God (more than the king), would not obey Pharaoh's murderous command; the fear of God the root of their refusal; the godly principle (the fear of God overruling the fear of man, the command of God above the command of the king); the conscience awed by God; the women who would not shed innocent blood, whatever the king commanded.

On "did not as the king of Egypt commanded them," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and the dominant reading note the disobedience — "did not as the king of Egypt commanded": the midwives refused to obey the king's wicked order (the holy disobedience to an unlawful command); the principle, "We ought to obey God rather than men" (Acts 5:29); the lawful refusal of a sinful command (the king's authority not extending to commanding murder); the conscientious resistance; the example of the godly (refusing the magistrate when he commands sin, Daniel 3:18; 6:10; Acts 4:19).

On "but saved the men children alive," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the deliverance: "but saved the men children alive" — the midwives saved the male infants (preserving them, contrary to the order); the saving of the children (and, among them, the future deliverers, the nation preserved); the providence (God using the women's fear to frustrate the genocide, and rewarding them, vv.20-21); the courage of faith. The verse displays the midwives fearing God rather than the king.

The witnesses agree the substance: the midwives, fearing God, disobeyed the king and saved the children (Calvin, Wesley, Henry, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of holy disobedience: "the midwives feared God, and did not as the king commanded" — the fear of God overruling the fear of man, the refusal of a sinful command, the principle "we ought to obey God rather than men" (Acts 5:29; Daniel 3:18); the king's authority not reaching to command murder; the courage of faith that saved the children and (under God) the nation. The verse displays the holy-disobedience crux. The verse marks the fear: of God. The verse displays the disobedience: to the king. The verse displays the saving: of the children. The verse displays the principle: obey God. The fear displays the midwives' godly resistance. The verse displays the midwives: fearing God. The verse displays the king: disobeyed. The verse displays the children: saved. The midwives fearing God rather than the king display the crux: they feared God, did not as the king commanded, and saved the children (Acts 5:29; Daniel 3:18) — the fear of God overruling the fear of man, the holy refusal of a murderous command. The verse displays the questioned midwives (v.18).
18:
"And the king of Egypt called for the midwives, and said unto them, Why have ye done this thing, and have saved the men children alive?" The very brief verse notes Pharaoh questioning the midwives. The grammatical voices note the inquiry. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the king of Egypt called for the midwives, and said unto them, Why have ye done this thing, and have saved the men children alive?" — Pharaoh, finding his order disobeyed (the male children living, v.17), called the midwives to account; the angry inquiry; the demand to know why they had saved the children; the confronting of the disobedient women.

On "Why have ye done this thing," the witnesses note the demand. Gill notes Pharaoh's demand (why the order was not carried out) — the king's displeasure; the calling to account; the danger to the midwives.

On "have saved the men children alive," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the disobedience: "have saved the men children alive" — the midwives' disobedience exposed (the children living); the king's anger; the providence (God protecting the midwives, who would answer, v.19, and be rewarded, vv.20-21); the failing of the murderous plot. The verse displays Pharaoh questioning the midwives.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the inquiry — "Why have ye done this thing, and have saved the men children alive?" the demand (v.17, Keil, Gill); "Why have ye done this thing," the displeasure (Gill); "have saved the men children alive," the disobedience (vv.20-21, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the questioning king. The verse marks the inquiry: why done this. The verse displays the midwives: called. The verse displays the children: saved. The verse displays the plot: failing. The inquiry displays Pharaoh confronting the midwives. The verse displays the king: questioning. The verse displays the midwives: accountable. The verse displays the providence: protecting. Pharaoh questioning the midwives displays the plot exposed and failing: why have ye saved the men children alive? (v.17) — the king's angry inquiry, the disobedience exposed, the providence protecting the God-fearing women. The verse displays the midwives' answer (v.19).
19:
"And the midwives said unto Pharaoh, Because the Hebrew women are not as the Egyptian women; for they are lively, and are delivered ere the midwives come in unto them." The witnesses note the midwives' answer to Pharaoh. On "the Hebrew women are not as the Egyptian women; for they are lively," the grammatical voices note the answer. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the Hebrew women are not as the Egyptian women; for they are lively, and are delivered ere the midwives come in unto them" — the midwives answered that the Hebrew women, being vigorous (lively, hardy), gave birth quickly, before the midwives could come; the explanation given to Pharaoh (whether wholly true, or a partial excuse to cover their refusal); the answer that turned aside the king's wrath.

On the answer, interpreters weigh its truth. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and the dominant reading note that the midwives' answer was likely true in part (the Hebrew women, vigorous from labor, often did deliver quickly) — yet the witnesses note that, if the midwives spoke not the whole truth (concealing their deliberate refusal), this was a fault (the Scripture commends their fear of God and saving of the children, not any dissembling); the lesson — the good they did (fearing God, saving life) was right and rewarded (vv.20-21), but any untruth was not to be imitated (the end not justifying a lie); the witnesses careful to distinguish the praised faith from any blamed deceit.

On "delivered ere the midwives come in unto them," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the providence: the answer (whether true or partly excusing) turned aside Pharaoh's wrath; the providence protecting the midwives (and the children); the failing of the plot; God overruling to preserve his people. The verse displays the midwives' answer to Pharaoh.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the answer — "the Hebrew women are not as the Egyptian women; for they are lively," the explanation (Keil, Gill); the truth of the answer (the Hebrew women vigorous, yet, if concealing their refusal, a fault not to be imitated, Wesley, the dominant reading); the providence (the wrath turned aside, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the answering midwives. The verse marks the answer: the women lively. The verse displays the explanation: quick birth. The verse displays the truth: in part. The verse displays the providence: protecting. The answer displays the midwives turning aside the wrath. The verse displays the midwives: answering. The verse displays the Pharaoh: turned aside. The verse displays the providence: at work. The midwives' answer to Pharaoh displays the providence protecting them: the Hebrew women are lively, delivered ere we come (vv.20-21) — the answer that turned aside the wrath; the witnesses commend their fear of God, not any dissembling. The verse displays the rewarded midwives (vv.20-21).
20:
"Therefore God dealt well with the midwives: and the people multiplied, and waxed very mighty." The witnesses note God rewarding the midwives. On "God dealt well with the midwives," the grammatical voices note the reward. Ellicott and Gill note that "God dealt well with the midwives" — God rewarded the midwives (dealt kindly with them) for their fear of him and their saving of the children; the divine approval of their godly conduct; the reward of faith (the fear of God blessed); the providence honoring those who feared him.

On "God dealt well with the midwives," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the approval. Wesley and Clarke note God's reward — not for any dissembling (v.19), but for their fear of God and their preserving of life; the divine commendation of their courage; the blessing on the God-fearing (Psalm 31:19); the reward (the building of their houses, v.21).

On "the people multiplied, and waxed very mighty," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the increase: "the people multiplied, and waxed very mighty" — Israel, despite the plot, multiplied and grew mighty (the genocide frustrated, the nation preserved); the providence overruling (the plot failing, the people growing); the covenant promise advancing (the great nation, Genesis 46:3). The verse displays God rewarding the midwives.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reward — "God dealt well with the midwives," the blessing (Ellicott, Gill); "God dealt well with the midwives," the approval (Psalm 31:19, Wesley, Clarke); "the people multiplied, and waxed very mighty," the increase (Genesis 46:3, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rewarded midwives. The verse marks the reward: dealt well. The verse displays the fear: of God blessed. The verse displays the people: multiplied. The verse displays the providence: overruling. The reward displays God honoring the God-fearing midwives. The verse displays the God: rewarding. The verse displays the midwives: blessed. The verse displays the people: mighty. God rewarding the midwives displays the blessing on the God-fearing: God dealt well with them, and the people multiplied (Psalm 31:19; Genesis 46:3) — the reward of their fear of God, the nation preserved and multiplied. The verse displays the building of their houses (v.21).
21:
"And it came to pass, because the midwives feared God, that he made them houses." The witnesses note God building the midwives' houses for their fear of him. On "because the midwives feared God," the grammatical voices note the reason. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "because the midwives feared God, that he made them houses" — the reason of the reward (v.20) restated: it was "because the midwives feared God" that he blessed them; the fear of God the ground of the blessing; the divine approval of the godly fear (the root of their courage); the principle (God honors those who fear him, 1 Samuel 2:30).

On "the midwives feared God," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the principle. Calvin and Ellicott note that the fear of God (the principle of their conduct) was what God rewarded — not the manner of their answer (v.19), but the godly fear that saved the children; the fear of God blessed; the principle of the reward.

On "he made them houses," the puritan and grammatical-historical note the blessing: "he made them houses" — God "made them houses" (built up their families, gave them households, posterity, prosperity; the "house" a family, as 2 Samuel 7:11); the reward of a settled, blessed family (the blessing on the God-fearing, Psalm 112:1-2); the providence (God honoring those who feared him above the king); the building of their houses for their faith. The verse displays God building the midwives' houses.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reason — "because the midwives feared God, that he made them houses," the fear of God rewarded (v.20; 1 Samuel 2:30, Keil, Gill); "the midwives feared God," the principle (Calvin, Ellicott); "he made them houses," the blessing (2 Samuel 7:11; Psalm 112:2, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessed midwives. The verse marks the fear: of God. The verse displays the reward: houses made. The verse displays the families: built up. The verse displays the principle: God honors his fearers. The blessing displays God rewarding the God-fearing midwives. The verse displays the midwives: blessed. The verse displays the houses: built. The verse displays the fear: rewarded. God building the midwives' houses for their fear of him displays the reward of godly fear: because they feared God, he made them houses (1 Samuel 2:30; Psalm 112:2) — the blessing of settled, prosperous families for those who feared God above the king. The verse displays Pharaoh's final edict (v.22).
22:
"And Pharaoh charged all his people, saying, Every son that is born ye shall cast into the river, and every daughter ye shall save alive." The witnesses note Pharaoh's open edict to drown the Hebrew sons. On "Pharaoh charged all his people," the grammatical voices note the edict. Ellicott and Gill note that "Pharaoh charged all his people, saying, Every son that is born ye shall cast into the river" — Pharaoh, the secret plot through the midwives having failed (vv.17-21), now issued an open, public command to all his people: to cast every newborn Hebrew son into the river (the Nile); the escalation to open genocide; the whole nation made the instrument of murder; the desperate cruelty.

On "Every son that is born ye shall cast into the river," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the cruelty. Calvin and Clarke note the drowning of the sons (in the Nile, the river the Egyptians worshipped, now made an instrument of murder) — the open, savage genocide; the rage against the seed (foreshadowing Herod, Matthew 2:16; the dragon, Revelation 12:4); the cruelty at its height.

On "every daughter ye shall save alive," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the providence: "every daughter ye shall save alive" — the females spared (for slaves, or wives to Egyptians, to absorb the nation); the cunning cruelty; yet the providence (the very river of death would, by God's hand, bear the deliverer Moses to safety, Exodus 2:3-10, the plot overruled for the deliverer's preservation); the rage that God would turn to the saving of the deliverer. The verse displays Pharaoh's open edict to drown the Hebrew sons.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the edict — "Every son that is born ye shall cast into the river," the open genocide (vv.17-21, Ellicott, Gill); "cast into the river," the cruelty (Matthew 2:16, Calvin, Clarke); "every daughter ye shall save alive," the providence (Exodus 2:3, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the murderous edict. The verse marks the edict: cast into the river. The verse displays the sons: drowned. The verse displays the daughters: spared. The verse displays the providence: to overrule. The edict displays Pharaoh's open genocide. The verse displays the Pharaoh: charging. The verse displays the river: of death. The verse displays the deliverer: to be borne. Pharaoh's open edict to drown the Hebrew sons displays the cruelty at its height: cast every son into the river (Matthew 2:16) — the open genocide, the river of death that God would make bear the deliverer to safety (Exodus 2:3-10). The verse closes the chapter, before the birth of Moses (ch.2).
